title: In Situ Growth of Polyaniline Nanofibers in Sulfonated UiO-66 MOF for High-Performance Symmetric Supercapacitor Devices
abstract_text: In modern energy catastrophes, supercapacitors are excellent energy storage devices, offering high power and energy density. Recently, metal organic framework (MOF)-based materials and their composites have gained significant attention as electrode materials in supercapacitor applications due to their high surface area and porosity, which assist the electrochemical process. However, their poor electrical conductivity limits their practical applications. To overcome this drawback, we incorporated a conducting polymer into a high surface area containing UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF to prepare a UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite using an in situ polymerization method. The −SO 3 H groups of the MOF acted as an internal proton source, enabling acid-free in situ PANI synthesis and producing a well-integrated composite with ameliorated symmetric supercapacitor performance in a two-electrode system. In the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, the MOF provided well-decorated porosity and surface area for electrical double layer capacitance (EDLC), while PANI introduced abundant electroactive sites and conductivity, enhancing the electron transfer process and pseudocapacitance. The in situ grown UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite exhibited a high specific capacitance of 1110 F g –1 at a current density of 1 A g –1 in a 1 M H 2 SO 4 electrolyte in a three-electrode system. The as-fabricated two-electrode symmetric supercapacitor device (SSCD) exhibited a specific capacitance of 150 F g –1 at a specific current density of 1 A g –1 , maintaining approximately 79% capacitance retention after 2000 continuous charge–discharge cycles. The SSC achieved a maximum power density of 5.3 kW kg –1 at 5 A g –1 and a maximum energy density of 20.9 Wh kg –1 at 1 A g –1 , with the capability to illuminate commercial LEDs.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.text: In modern energy catastrophes, supercapacitors are excellent energy storage devices, offering high power and energy density. Recently, metal organic framework (MOF)-based materials and their composites have gained significant attention as electrode materials in supercapacitor applications due to their high surface area and porosity, which assist the electrochemical process. However, their poor electrical conductivity limits their practical applications. To overcome this drawback, we incorporated a conducting polymer into a high surface area containing UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF to prepare a UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite using an in situ polymerization method. The −SO 3 H groups of the MOF acted as an internal proton source, enabling acid-free in situ PANI synthesis and producing a well-integrated composite with ameliorated symmetric supercapacitor performance in a two-electrode system. In the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, the MOF provided well-decorated porosity and surface area for electrical double layer capacitance (EDLC), while PANI introduced abundant electroactive sites and conductivity, enhancing the electron transfer process and pseudocapacitance. The in situ grown UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite exhibited a high specific capacitance of 1110 F g –1 at a current density of 1 A g –1 in a 1 M H 2 SO 4 electrolyte in a three-electrode system. The as-fabricated two-electrode symmetric supercapacitor device (SSCD) exhibited a specific capacitance of 150 F g –1 at a specific current density of 1 A g –1 , maintaining approximately 79% capacitance retention after 2000 continuous charge–discharge cycles. The SSC achieved a maximum power density of 5.3 kW kg –1 at 5 A g –1 and a maximum energy density of 20.9 Wh kg –1 at 1 A g –1 , with the capability to illuminate commercial LEDs.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.text: 1 Introduction The development of alternative energy sources is crucial for mitigating the increasing demand for electricity today. The increasing environmental threats from fossil fuel combustion, combined with the ongoing energy demand for an enhanced population, have made it essential to explore alternative solutions for renewable and clean energy production for daily use. The rapid growth of electrochemical energy storage devices in the energy sector has improved the situation for clean and sustainable energy, helping to overcome the energy crisis. Among the various energy storage devices, electrical energy storage devices have garnered significant attention due to their wide range of applications in portable electronic devices and electric vehicles. , In this regard, supercapacitors are extensively used as a secondary energy storage technology due to their high power density, decent energy density, longer life spans, good reversibility properties, and exceptional intrinsic properties, with uninterrupted power supply, which will fill the gaps between the batteries and conventional capacitors. − Researchers came across various materials used as electrode materials in supercapacitors, including transition metal oxides/hydroxides, , metal organic frameworks (MOFs) or porous solid-state materials, − porous carbon, conductive polymers, etc. Supercapacitors are classified as electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors based on the electrochemical energy storage process. Most carbon-based materials follow the EDLC principle, as they electrostatically store charge at the electrode–electrolyte interface. On the other hand, pseudocapacitors exhibit capacitive-like electrochemical behavior by storing charge in the electrode in response to surface redox processes. , This behavior is observed in most metal oxides and conductive polymers, where redox-active sites are prevalent. EDLC behavior is predominant in materials with high surface areas. However, the amalgamation of EDLC and pseudocapacitive behavior in a single material can provide enhanced energy storage properties due to synergistic effects. MOFs are a class of metal–organic hybrid materials composed of metal nodes and organic linkers, which form porous, crystalline structures with unique properties. According to recent studies, MOFs are currently being explored and developed for use in energy storage systems due to their large surface area and tunable pore structures. Multiple functionalities and versatile syntheses of MOFs have been intriguing materials for electrochemical energy storage systems, particularly as supercapacitors. However, they still exhibit poor electrical conductivity, which limits their application as electrode materials in supercapacitors. − To overcome this constraint, novel MOFs fine-tuned with enhanced intrinsic conductivity by conducting polymers or fillers are essential. − Among the intrinsically conducting polymers, polyaniline is one of the most prominently used composite materials due to its high conductivity, environmental stability, and ease of synthesis. A homogeneous PANI polymer composite with a high surface area and porous MOF can enhance the supercapacitive properties due to the synergistic combination of both EDLC and pseudocapacitive properties. In a polymer composite, in situ polymer composite formation is generally considered superior to ex-situ physical mixing of the components, primarily because it achieves better dispersion of the filler material within the polymer matrix and enhances the interfacial bonding between the components. Herein, we have employed the sulfonic acid-containing UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF, which features a very high surface area and regular porosity, coupled with an inherent proton source from the −SO 3 H groups, allowing for the protonation of aniline monomers and promoting oxidative polymerization to produce an in situ UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite. This self-doping mechanism not only simplifies the synthesis by eliminating the need for an external acid dopant but also facilitates the uniform distribution of the MOF throughout the PANI matrix. As a consequence, the high interfacial interaction between PANI and the SO 3 H-MOF improves the structural integration and may enhance the composite’s charge transport properties. Shao and colleagues documented the Zr-MOF-PANI composite as an electrode material using 1 M H 2 SO 4 as the electrolyte, achieving a specific capacitance of 1015 F/g at a current density of 1 A g –1 . In another example, an in situ grown UiO-66-NH 2 –PANI composite exhibited a high specific capacitance of 462.2 F g –1 at a specific current of 1 A g –1 in a 2 M KOH electrolyte. In our research, the developed 1:1 w/w UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite exhibited a specific capacitance of 1110 F g –1 , maintaining its capacitance up to 2000 cycles when measured in 1 M H 2 SO 4 as the electrolyte. The symmetric supercapacitor device (SSD) using UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI as an electrode achieved a specific capacitance of 150 F/g at a current density of 1 A g –1 . Moreover, it exhibits a maximum power density of 5.3 kW kg –1 at 5 A g –1 and a maximum energy density of 20.9 Wh kg –1 at 1 A g –1 . Thus, the demonstrated superior energy storage performance of UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI, particularly in symmetric supercapacitor devices, indicates its strong potential as an electrode material for supercapacitors.

sections.title: Experimental Section
sections.text: 2 Experimental Section 2.1 Materials All of the chemicals used were of analytical grade. Zirconium oxide (IV) chloride (ZrCl 4 ) (98%, Avra), terephthalic acid (H 2 BDC) (98%, Avra), monosodium 2-sulfoterephthalate (98%, TCI), aniline (99.5%, SRL), ammonium persulfate (APS) (99%, SRL), hydrochloric acid (HCl) (34%, Avra), N , N -dimethylformamide (DMF) (99%, SRL), acetic acid (CH 3 COOH, 99.9%), sulfuric acid (H 2 SO 4 ), and methanol were all purchased from Hychem Laboratories, Hyderabad, India.

sections.title: Synthesis of UiO-66 SO 3 H
sections.text: 2.2 Synthesis of UiO-66 SO 3 H UiO-66 SO 3 H was synthesized as per the procedure reported in the literature with slight modifications. First, ZrCl 4 (0.466 g), monosodium 2-sulfoterephthalate (0.134 g), and H 2 BDC (0.249 g) were dissolved in 64 mL of DMF. Approximately 8 mL of HCl and CH 3 COOH were added to the above solution and sonicated for 30 min until the solution was fully dissolved. Then, the solution was kept in a closed vessel and heated for 24 h at 80 °C in a hot air oven. The precipitate of UiO-66 SO 3 H particles was collected by centrifugation, followed by washing with ethanol and DMF three times, and dried at 80 °C for 12 h.

sections.title: Synthesis of PANI
sections.text: 2.3 Synthesis of PANI Polyaniline was synthesized according to the reported literature. About 0.93 g of aniline was dissolved in 30 mL of 1 M HCl to form solution (A). Separately, 2.28 g of APS was dissolved in 20 mL of 1 M HCl to prepare solution (B). Both solutions were precooled at 0–4 °C for 20 min. Solution (A) was then added to solution (B) under rapid stirring, and the polymerization reaction was allowed to proceed for 30 min. The resulting deep green precipitate was collected, washed five times with ethanol and distilled water, and dried overnight at 60 °C.

sections.title: Synthesis of In Situ UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI Composite
sections.text: 2.4 Synthesis of In Situ UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI Composite UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite was synthesized by the in situ synthesis method. About 21.4 mg of the synthesized UiO-66 SO 3 H was added into 21.9 mg of aniline with constant stirring at 0 to 5 °C. A portion of 53.6 mg of APS dissolved in 1.8 mL of water was added to the aniline solution dropwise with constant stirring for 24 h. The deep-green solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm, using ethanol and water for washing, and the process was repeated 10 times. Finally, the composite was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h to get a green UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite. The schematic representation of the MOF-PANI composite synthesis is shown in Scheme . 1 Schematic Representation of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI Composite

sections.title: Preparation of Electrodes
sections.text: 2.5 Preparation of Electrodes A carbon plate (3 cm × 1 cm) was used as a current collector, and NMP was used as a solvent. The slurry was prepared by mixing 10 mg of active material (UiO-66 SO 3 H or UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite), 2 mg of activated carbon, 20 μL of Nafion, and 500 μL of NMP. The slurry was sonicated for 12 h and then drop-casted a 50 μL portion onto the carbon plate in a 1 cm 2 area to prepare the MOF- or MOF-composite-modified electrode, with a mass loading of 1 mg/cm 2 (mass ratio of 10:2 w/w between active materials and activated carbon). The mass loading of the material was 1 mg. It was then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

sections.title: Device Fabrication
sections.text: 2.6 Device Fabrication An SSD was constructed by using a UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite as both the positive and negative electrodes. The symmetric supercapacitor electrode (two-electrode study) was fabricated by an identical electrode with an active material of 0.1 mg from the above-mentioned recipe on each electrode, resulting in a positive to negative mass ratio of 1:1. A PVA-H 2 SO 4 gel-based membrane was used as the separator electrolyte, prepared by dissolving 1.5 g of PVA in 10 mL of 1 M H 2 SO 4 and stirring at 85 °C until a clear gel was formed. Then, the gel was poured into a Petri dish and dried in an oven to remove excess water. The gel was solidified and then peeled off. According to the electrode size, the gel electrolyte film was cut into small pieces. The SSD was fabricated by sandwiching a solidified gel electrolyte film between two pieces of UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite-modified carbon plate electrodes. The electrochemical properties of this device were studied in a two-electrode system.

sections.title: Material Characterizations
sections.text: 2.7 Material Characterizations The FTIR spectra of UiO-66 SO 3 H and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites were obtained in the range of 4000–500 cm –1 in the ATR method by using Bruker Alpha II Platinum ATR. The UV–vis spectroscopic studies were carried out using a JASCO V-670 spectrometer by dissolving UiO-66 SO 3 H and its composite in ethanol at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. The PXRD data were recorded using a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer for all samples, with constant parameters of 0–70° and a scan rate of 2°/min. Morphological studies were conducted by using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) with a FEI ApreoS FESEM instrument, operating at 20 kV, by drop-casting the material onto copper tape. Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) was performed by drop-casting the material onto copper tape and drying the sample at room temperature. The surface area of the composites was confirmed by nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms. The thermal stability of the MOF and its composites was analyzed using a Shimadzu DTG-60 TGA instrument in the temperature range of 30 to 800 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min –1 under an inert N 2 atmosphere.

sections.title: Electrochemical Characterizations
sections.text: 2.8 Electrochemical Characterizations All electrochemical analyses, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), were conducted on freshly prepared materials in a 1 M H 2 SO 4 electrolyte using a three-electrode system. A carbon plate was used as a current collector, while Ag/AgCl in saturated KCl and Pt wire were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The electrochemical performance of the electrodes and SSD was analyzed using an OrigaFlex OGF500 electrochemical workstation. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed at different scan rates, ranging from 20 to 100 mV/s, with a potential window of 0 to 0.8 V. The capacitance values were derived from GCD discharge cycles. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed over a frequency range of 10 kHz to 0.01 Hz with an AC amplitude of 5 mV. The formulas used for calculating specific capacitance, energy, and power density for two- and three-electrode systems are presented in the Supporting Information .

sections.title: Results and Discussions
sections.text: 3 Results and Discussions The XRD patterns of pure MOF, PANI, and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites are shown in Figure a. The XRD of pure MOF matches the reported literature, which clearly indicates the successful synthesis and crystallinity of UiO-66 SO 3 H. The XRD pattern matches the simulated XRD pattern of UiO-66 in Figure S1a in the Supporting Information SI , indicating that the crystal structure of the MOF is not affected by sulfonic acid-containing linkers. PANI peaks were observed at 25.5° and 19.5°, attributed to its semicrystalline nature. The characteristic diffraction peaks of the pristine UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF and PANI were observed in the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, attributing to the successful interaction of the UiO-66 SO 3 H and PANI in the composite without destroying the crystallinity of the UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF. 1 (a) XRD pattern; (b) FTIR spectra; (c) UV–vis spectra; and (d) nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of pure UiO-66 SO 3 H, PANI, and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites. The FTIR spectra of pure UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF and the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite are shown in Figure b. Pure MOF shows a characteristic broad peak of the O–H stretching at 3000–3500 cm –1 . The peak at 1579 cm –1 is attributed to asymmetric C–O stretching of the COO– carboxylate coordinated to zirconium. A typical asymmetric stretching of SO was observed at 1227 cm –1 , accompanied by a symmetric stretching of SO at 1176 cm –1 . The peak at 1079 cm –1 is attributed to the C–S stretching vibration. The peak at 746 cm –1 corresponds to an aromatic C–H out-of-plane bending from the linker, which contains a benzene ring. The Zr–O bending vibration was observed at 655 and 551 cm –1 due to Zr–O clusters. , Pure PANI exhibits a characteristic peak at 1562 cm –1 , attributed to quinoid CC stretching. The benzenoid form of CC stretching was observed at 1479 cm –1 . C–N stretching of secondary aromatic amine showed a peak at 1295 cm –1 . The peak at 1134 cm –1 may correspond to C–N + stretching vibration, and the peak at 797 cm –1 corresponds to the C–H bending mode of benzenoid or quinoid rings, which confirms that the polymer is in the emeraldine salt form. , The formation of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite was confirmed by the appearance of the peaks related to pure UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF and PANI, which clearly indicates the successful polymerization of aniline in the presence of −SO 3 H functionalized UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF. The UV–vis spectra of the pure UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF, PANI, and the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite are shown in Figure c. The two characteristic peaks of PANI, which appeared at 284 and 476 nm, were due to the π–π* and polaron−π* transitions in quinoid and benzenoid units. The broad peak observed at 700 to 1000 nm is due to π–polarons transition, which confirms the emeraldine salt conductive form of PANI. The pure UiO-66 SO 3 H showed the peak at 250 nm, which is due to the π–π* transition of the aromatic ring of the aromatic linkers. For the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, the peak demonstrates a blue shift at 224 and 240 nm. The quinoid and benzenoid peaks of PANI are also blue-shifted in the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite. Again, the similar broad peak observed at 700 to 1000 nm is due to the π-polaron transition in the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, implying the highly conducting emeraldine salt form of PANI in the composite. The peak shifts correlate with the interactions of the MOF with PANI, as supported by FTIR. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis was used to determine the specific surface area and pore size distribution of pure MOF, PANI, and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites, as shown in Figure d. The pristine MOF showed the highest specific surface area of 872 m 2 g –1 with a typical type II isotherm with a microporous metal organic framework with a pore size of 1.179 nm. Pure PANI showed a type III isotherm with a surface area of 32 m 2 g –1 . The UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite displayed a specific surface area of 68 m 2 g –1 with a type III isotherm. The BET surface area of the composites decreased with the incorporation of PANI, as expected for composite materials. This reduction is attributed to the partial filling of the MOF pores by PANI chains during composite formation. The micro- and mesopores of the MOF were filled with PANI in the composites, resulting in a decrease in pore volume. The reduction in average pore width from 6.2 to 3.9 nm, as shown in Figure S1b and Table S1 in Supporting Information , indicates that PANI chains were successfully incorporated within the pores and on the surface of UiO-66 SO 3 H, leading to partial pore filling and structural integration between the MOF and the conducting polymer. However, there can be a trade-off between surface area and conductivity. As the surface area decreases, the composite’s conductivity and electrochemical activity improve. This enhancement is attributed to the synergy between electrical conductivity and the polymer’s introduction of redox-active sites, which facilitates fast electron transfer, improves charge transfer at the electrode–electrolyte interface, and, most importantly, contributes to pseudocapacitance through reversible redox reactions. The morphologies of pure UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF, PANI, and their composites were examined using FESEM. The pure UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF exhibits an octahedral architecture with aggregated particles, as depicted in Figure a. PANI exhibits a nanotubular morphology with a diameter of 200–300 nm, as observed in Figure b. In the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, noticeable morphological variations were observed, with homogeneous nanofibers decorated with UiO-66 SO 3 H, as shown in Figure c. The nanofiber diameter is drastically reduced in the case of the composite, as the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite exhibits a diameter of less than 100 nm. The change in diameter confirms reduced aggregation of the PANI chains, as they are decorated with the UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF in the composite structure. The EDX elemental mapping ( Figure d–i) reveals a homogeneous distribution of primary elements, such as C, O, N, S, and Zr, on the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite. To calculate elemental percentages, EDX analysis was performed ( Figure j). The UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI exhibits the following elemental compositions: carbon, 87.3%; nitrogen, 1.04%; oxygen, 8.92%; sulfur, 2.10%; and zirconium, 0.60%. Carbon is predominantly present in PANI and MOF, and its elemental analysis with mapping is shown in Figures S2 and S3 in the SI . The thermal stability of the MOF-PANI composite is evidenced in Figure S4 in the SI . The initial weight loss observed at 100–200 was attributed to the loss of water and solvent molecules in the sample. The weight loss from 300 to 800 °C is due to the decomposition of organic linkers in the MOF, accompanied by a breakdown of the polymer backbone. The increase in polyaniline decreased the thermal stability. The composite showed improved thermal stability due to strong interactions between the MOF and PANI. 2 (a) FESEM analysis of UiO-66 SO 3 H, (b) PANI, and (c) UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites; (d–i) elemental mapping of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite; the EDX electron mapping of UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI for (d) overall, (e) C, (f) N, (g) O, (h) S, and (i) Zr; and (j) EDX elemental composition of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite. XPS studies of PANI, pure MOF, and the MOF-PANI composite ( Figures S5–S6 in Supporting Information ) were performed to investigate bonding states and surface chemical composition and to study the interfacial interaction between the MOF-PANI composites. The survey spectrum of pure PANI confirms the presence of C, N, and O ( Figure S5 ). The fitted spectra of carbon 1s, as shown in Figure S6a , show the peaks at 284.5 eV and 285.4 eV, corresponding to CC and C–C of benzenoid rings of PANI, and the peaks at 286.8 and 288.4 eV correspond to C–N and CN groups associated with the quinoid imine structure with the π–π* satellite peak. The N 1s spectra ( Figure S6b ) exhibit three characteristic peaks at 399.5, 400.7, and 402 eV, attributed to (N) imine, amine (−NH−), and protonated NH + , respectively, which confirm the partial protonation of PANI with its emeraldine salt structure. The O 1s spectra ( Figure S6c ) show peaks at 531.5 and 532.8 eV, attributed to carbonyl groups (CO) in quinoids and to C–O bonds and to physically adsorbed water molecules on the surface. The pristine UiO-66 SO 3 H survey spectrum reveals the presence of C, O, S, and Zr as the primary elements ( Figure S4 ). The C 1s spectrum ( Figure S6d ) exhibits peaks at 284.6, 286.5, 288.3, and 289.4 eV assigned to C–C/CC bonds of the organic linker and the peaks at higher binding energies, 288.3 and 289.4 eV, attributed to OCO groups coordinated to Zr nodes. The O 1s spectra in Figure S6e show characteristic peaks at 529.5, 531.6, 533.5, and 535.7 eV, corresponding to Zr–O and Zr–O–C bonding. The sulfur doublet peaks ( Figure S6f ) correspond to 167.8 eV (S 2p 3/2 ) and 169.4 eV (S 2p 1/2 ), confirming the incorporation of the SO 3 H group into the MOF. The UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite shows C, N, O, S, and Zr as the primary elements ( Figure S5 ). In the composite, noticeable shifts in binding energies have been observed due to strong interfacial interactions between the MOF and PANI. The C 1s spectra of the MOF-PANI composite ( Figure S6g ) exhibit peaks at 284, 284.8, 286.3, and 288.7 eV, indicating that the electronic environment of carbon changed upon the introduction of the polymer. The N 1s spectra ( Figure S6h ) reveal the peaks at higher binding energies of 399.4 eV, 401.4 eV, and 404.1 eV, confirming the successful protonation of PANI without the use of external acid, which clearly indicates the in situ synthesis of PANI by sulfonated MOF with strong electrostatic interaction between protonated PANI and immobilized sulfonate anions, evidencing efficient interfacial charge transfer between UiO-66 SO 3 H and PANI. The core-level O 1s spectra ( Figure S6i ) show peaks at 530.4, 531.7, and 533.2 eV, indicating the retention of Zr–O clusters and sulfonate oxygen. The similar shift in core-level S 2p XPS spectra of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, as shown in Figure S6j , confirms the composite formation between MOF and PANI. The electrical conductivity of PANI, UiO-66 SO 3 H, and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites was measured by preparing a pellet using the four-probe method, as shown in Figure S7 in the Supporting Information . The pure MOF exhibits a very low electrical conductivity of 1.2 × 10 –3 S m –1 owing to the absence of continuous electron-transport pathways and the intrinsically insulating nature of the organic linkers. However, PANI exhibits high conductivity due to its highly conjugated π-electron backbone, which enabled efficient charge delocalization and significantly improved conductivity to 3 × 10 1 S m –1 . Upon the formation of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, a huge enhancement of electrical conductivity of the MOF is exhibited, with an electrical conductivity of 1.1 × 10 1 m –1 . The conductivity parameters of three compounds are summarized in Table S2 in the SI . This improvement in electrical conductivity is attributed to a synergistic interaction between the MOF and PANI, which facilitated interfacial contact and generated continuous conductive pathways in the composite. 3.1 Electrochemical Properties The primary evaluation of the electrochemical performance of UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite was conducted in a three-electrode system, where a carbon plate modified electrode served as the working electrode, with Ag/AgCl in saturated KCl functioning as the reference electrode (RE), a Pt wire acting as the counter electrode (CE), and 1 M H 2 SO 4 used as the electrolyte. , The CV analysis was performed within a potential window of 0 to 0.8 V for both pristine UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, as shown in Figure a. The UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite-modified electrode displays a higher enclosed area in the CV plot than that of the pristine MOF, indicating better performance for the composite. The EDLC and pseudocapacitive properties of MOF and PANI are confirmed by the nonfaradaic region and faradaic redox peaks in the CV, respectively. Figure b displays the characteristic voltammogram of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite-modified electrode at different scan rates, showing the reversible redox behavior of the electrode material with oxidation and reduction peaks at 0.53 and 0.29 V, respectively, at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The CV and GCD of UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF are depicted in Figure S8a-b in the SI 3 (a) CV comparison of UiO-66 SO 3 H and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites in a three-electrode system at a scan rate of 50 mV/s, (b) CV of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite at different scan rates from 20 to 100 mV/s, (c) b values from the anodic and cathodic peaks of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, (d) capacitive and diffusive contributions of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite at different scan rates, (e) GCD of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite at different current densities, (f) GCD and (g) specific capacitance comparison of UiO-66 SO 3 H and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites at 1 A g –1 , (h) EIS of UiO-66 SO 3 H and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites, and (i) cyclic stability of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite. The UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite exhibits significantly improved electrochemical performance owing to the addition of redox-active PANI. This shows a well-defined redox peak with an enhanced current response, indicating effective charge transportation in the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites. Additionally, the peak current is well fitted linearly with the square root of the scan rate, indicating that the electrochemical process is diffusion-controlled and exhibits predominantly pseudocapacitive behavior. The mechanism of the electrochemical performance was analyzed using the following equations: − 1 i = a ν b 2 log ( i ) = log ⁡ a + b log ( ν ) where i is the peak current, ν represents the scan rate, and a and b are the adjustable parameters. Figure c shows that the average b value is around 0.61, indicating prominent diffusive behavior with moderate EDLC contributions. The overall diffusion and surface capacitive contributions are evaluated using the following equations: − 3 i = k 1 v + k 2 v 1 / 2 4 k 1 v i + k 2 v 1 / 2 i = 1 Here, k 1 and k 2 are constants, and their values can be calculated from i ν 1 / 2 versus the square root of the scan rate plot, as shown in Figure S9 in the SI . From this equation, the capacitive contribution is calculated by k 1 ν i and the diffusion-controlled contribution evaluated by k 2 v 1 / 2 i . The calculated k 1 and k 2 values are 0.0702 and 0.0664, respectively. Accordingly, the calculated contribution of the diffusion-controlled pseudocapacitive process at a scan rate of 20 mV/s is ∼88%, which decreases to 76% at 100 mV/s. Consequently, the surface capacitive or EDLC contribution was calculated to be 12% at 20 mV/s and 24% at 100 mV/s, as shown in Figure d. The GCD curves of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite electrode at different current densities are shown in Figure e. The nature of the CV and GCD clearly indicates the material’s hybrid behavior, in which the redox reaction not only involves a surface redox process but also involves ion intercalation, as evidenced by the double-layer capacitance. The specific capacitance values for the pristine UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite electrodes are determined based on their GCD curves and compared ( Figure f). The detailed CV and GCD studies of the pure MOF are shown in Figure S4 of the SI . The composite exhibits nearly two times higher capacitance than the bare UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF at a current density of 1 A g –1 . Notably, the value of specific capacitance for the UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF is measured as 630 F g –1 , whereas the same is determined as 1110 F g –1 for the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites in Figure f-g, which is very much comparable and superior to the other MOF-based supercapacitors reported earlier ( Table S3 in the Supporting Information ). The transport properties of MOF and MOF-PANI composites were evaluated by an EIS study. The Nyquist plots of UiO-66 SO 3 H and UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite-modified electrodes were measured in a frequency range from 10 kHz to 0.01 Hz and are depicted in Figure h. From the fitting of the Nyquist plot using the equivalent circuit given in Figure h, the EIS parameters are evaluated. The solution resistances ( R S ) for both systems are very low. The charge-transfer resistances ( R CT ) are calculated to be 7 and 8.5 Ω for the pure MOF and the composite, respectively. For the composite electrodes, the R CT and the intrinsic resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface are reduced, indicating better charge/electron transport at the composite/electrolyte interface compared to the pure MOF. The composite’s better performance is also supported by its lower R CT compared to the pure MOF. In the low-frequency region, the straight line indicates the diffusive resistance of OH – ions, corresponding to a Warburg impedance (W) of 76.139 mS. The very low value of the constant phase element (CPE = 235.44 mS) indicates a less rough electrode surface, good homogeneity, and a highly porous electrode material. The phase angle in the Nyquist plot is nearly 45°, indicating a stronger diffusion contribution to the supercapacitor behavior and improved ion mobility of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite. The power and energy density of both materials in the three-electrode system are depicted in Figure h. Additionally, the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite-modified electrode exhibits high durability, retaining 71% of its initial value after 2000 cycles at 5 A g –1 , as shown in Figure i. To investigate the effects of PANI in different composite ratios on the electrochemical performance of the material in the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, composites with different PANI ratios (25% and 50% w/w) were synthesized and evaluated. The electrode containing 25% PANI in the composite exhibits a lower capacitance of 761 F g –1 ( Figure S10 in the Supporting Information ) than the 50% PANI-containing UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite (1110 F g –1 ), due to insufficient electroactive sites and limited conductive pathways. In contrast, the 50% PANI containing the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite significantly enhanced the electrochemical performance, attributed to improved synergistic interactions among the components, increased active surface area, and enhanced charge-transport kinetics. Consequently, the 50% PANI containing the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite was selected as the optimal composition for subsequent electrochemical studies.

sections.title: Symmetric Supercapacitor Properties
sections.text: 3.2 Symmetric Supercapacitor Properties The remarkable supercapacitive properties of composite electrodes in the three-electrode system prompted us to investigate their potential as a Symmetric Supercapacitor (SSC) in a two-electrode system. The synthesized UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite was used as both a positive and a negative electrode. Voltammograms were recorded at scan rates ranging from 20 to 100 mV/s within a fixed potential window of 0 to 1 V, as shown in Figure a. The CV curve indicates that the well-retained shape persists with an increasing scan rate; the increase in anodic and cathodic peak currents is attributed to the ideal capacitive behavior of the SSC. 4 (a) CV of a two-electrode system SSC device; (b) GCD and (c) specific capacitance of the SSC device at different current densities; (d) EIS Nyquist plot and corresponding equivalent circuit of the SSC device; (e) Ragone plot to show the power and energy densities of the SSC device; and (f) cycle stability of the SSC device. The GCD of the SSC device was measured at current densities ranging from 1 to 5 Ag –1 , as shown in Figure b. The SSC device achieved the highest capacitance of 150 F/g at a current density of 1 A g –1 . The specific capacitance values at different current densities are shown in Figure c. The nonlinear GCD curve at different current densities further confirms the predominant pseudocapacitive nature of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite in the two-electrode SSC device. The transport properties of the symmetric supercapacitor device were investigated using EIS over the frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 10 kHz, as illustrated in Figure d. The equivalent circuit is presented in the inset of the figure. A small semicircle is observed in the high-frequency region, with a charge transfer resistance ( R CT ) of 3 Ω, indicating the composite’s low resistivity. The system exhibits a Warburg impedance (W) coefficient of 288.49 μS and a very low constant phase element (CPE) value of 61.15 μS. The enhanced ion diffusion was attributed to the combination of low R CT and a phase angle with the presence of intrinsic porosity in the MOF. The power and energy density of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite-based SSC device, as shown in the Ragone plot in Figure e, were compared with those reported in the literature in Table S4 in the Supporting Information . , − The symmetric supercapacitor achieved a maximum power density of 5.3 kW kg –1 at 5 A g –1 and a maximum energy density of 20.9 Wh kg –1 at 1 A g –1 . The enhancement of conductivity in the MOF system by utilizing PANI as a conducting polymer filler enhanced the energy storage parameters substantially, as shown in Table S3–S4 in Supporting Information . Furthermore, the cyclic stability of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite, as shown in Figure f, indicated the retention of 79% of the initial capacitance for up to 2000 charge–discharge cycles in the GCD. Furthermore, for practical applications, a solid-state SSC device was fabricated by using a carbon plate as the current collector and a PVA-H 2 SO 4 gel as the electrolyte. The schematic representation of the device and the fabricated device is shown in Figure a. Three devices were connected in series, which were useful for illuminating different LEDs at a voltage of ∼2.7 V, as described in Figure b-c. First, the SSC device was charged by using a 3 V battery for a few minutes. Then, using the charged SSC devices in series, different LEDs (red (∼1.8 V), green (∼2.1 V), blue (∼2.4 V), and orange (∼2 V)) were illuminated, as in Figure d–g. With consistent charging, the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite-based SSC device assembly can power a blue LED for 2 min, a green LED for 3 min, and a red LED for 2 min, all with sufficient brightness ( Figure S11 in the SI ). The practical application of the symmetric supercapacitor device confirmed the excellent electrochemical energy storage performance of the prepared UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite in solid-state devices, demonstrating its suitability for real-life energy applications. 5 (a) Schematic illustration of a solid-state SSC device by using UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI; (b) fabricated devices in a series connection; (c) device voltage after charging at 3 V for a few minutes; (d–g) different LED light illuminations using the solid-state SSCs in a series connection.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.text: 4 Conclusion The UiO-66 SO 3 H MOF, PANI, and in situ grown UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composites were successfully synthesized by in situ polymerization using an SO 3 H-functionalized UiO-66 MOF as an intrinsic proton source. The introduction of PANI by the in situ method into UiO-66 SO 3 H enhanced the overall electrochemical performance, supporting both diffusion-based pseudocapacitive behavior and the MOF’s inherent surface capacitive behavior. The specific capacitance of the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite film reached its highest value, 1110 F g –1 at 1 A g –1 , with low charge transfer resistance and good cycle stability. The solid-state SSC device fabricated by using the UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite exhibited a specific capacitance of 150 F g –1 at a current density of 1 A g –1 , a decent maximum power density of 5.3 kW kg –1 at 5 A g –1 , and a maximum energy density of 20.9 Wh kg –1 at 1 A g –1 with sufficient durability. The UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI composite-based SSC device could illuminate multiple LEDs for several minutes, demonstrating the effectiveness of UiO-66 SO 3 H-PANI as an electrode material for supercapacitor applications in the real world.
